title: Oldenlandia pseudocorymbosa
taxonomy: Oldenlandia pseudocorymbosa belongs to the family Rubiaceae, which is a large and diverse family of flowering plants commonly known as the madder family. This family consists of over 13,000 species and is one of the largest families of flowering plants in the world. The genus Oldenlandia is a small group of herbs and shrubs that are mainly found in tropical and subtropical regions. Oldenlandia pseudocorymbosa is native to certain parts of Asia, where it grows in a variety of habitats. The classification of Oldenlandia pseudocorymbosa is further supported by its morphological characteristics, which include its leaf shape and arrangement, as well as its flower structure.
uses: Oldenlandia pseudocorymbosa has been studied for its potential medicinal properties, including its use in treating various types of fever and infections. Its leaves and stems are traditionally used in folk medicine to alleviate symptoms of respiratory issues and colds. Some research suggests that Oldenlandia pseudocorymbosa may possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which could contribute to its medicinal uses. However, further studies are needed to confirm its efficacy and safety for human consumption. The plant is also valued in traditional Asian medicine for its ability to promote digestion and relieve gastrointestinal issues.

intro: Oldenlandia pseudocorymbosa, also known as Psammolaena, is a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in medicine to exhibit potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, which may aid in the treatment of various infections and inflammatory conditions. Bioactive constituents of Oldenlandia pseudocorymbosa include flavonoids and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Oldenlandia pseudocorymbosa is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and is commonly used to treat fever, cough, and skin conditions.
